When we were here before — Phase C reference periods Both resolved into Phase D when monetary conditions eased
2019 Q1–Q2 4 months

Post-2018 Q4 selloff. Fed had hiked 9 times and then signaled a pause.

Resolved: Phase D unlocked when Fed pivoted — first rate cut signal in July 2019 broke the stall.

Pattern: SPX consolidated above 200D for months, then broke higher once cut narrative solidified.

Today: Same rate pressure dynamic: Fed has begun cutting but 10Y remains elevated above 4.2%.

2023 H2 3 months

Post-regional bank stress. Inflation moderating but Fed still restrictive at 5.25%.

Resolved: Phase D unlocked when market priced in 2024 cuts — 10Y peaked at ~5% in October 2023.

Pattern: SPX tested 200D twice and held both times. Rally began when rate expectations turned.

Today: Same resolution mechanism: watch for 10Y sustained move below 4.0% and VIX below 15.

Phase C context

No significant signal today — framework holds.

Strategy read

Watching, not acting. SPX is 3.0% above the add zone (7,276–7,494) — if the pullback continues, that is the prepared entry level, not a reason to act now. Triggers: add zone 7,276–7,494 · defense 6,903 · VIX 20 posture review · HY OAS 3.5% phase warning. No triggers hit — no action.

Full analysis — 2022 vs 2025 comparison, historical analogs, decision brief

2022 bear vs 2025 correction — normalized from peak

Both anchored at their respective peaks, normalized to 0. Shows trajectory depth relative to 2022. Note: different causal regimes — 2022 was Fed-driven structural tightening; 2025 was tariff/sentiment shock with a different resolution mechanism. Illustrative, not predictive.

Historical analogs

1998Funding stress without immediate broad collapse
78%

Watch funding markets and dollar stress carefully; contained credit can delay but not eliminate risk repricing.

1969–70Tight money slowdown
74%

High money restriction can matter with a lag; avoid assuming resilience means no downside.

1973–74Oil shock / physical constraint recession
68%

Physical constraints can convert inflation into margin pressure; energy and infrastructure exposure may matter.

Narrative vs. Reality · Jun 9

Narrative gaps

AI Hardware Supercycle Data ahead
AVGONVDATSMASML

The market read the minor software miss as a capex-peak signal. The AI hardware data disagrees: $10.8B AI revenue in Q2 followed by >200% guided growth in Q3 is not a plateau. The custom ASIC order book of >$70B from Google, Meta, and Apple represents years of revenue already contracted. The -3% dip is mispricing. The entry window is open now before Q3 results confirm the acceleration.

Watch AVGO Q3 results (Sept 2026): AI revenue confirm >$12B quarter

Defense / Nuclear Backlog Data ahead
BWXTLEUCCJOKLO

BWXT is not an SMR speculation — it is a legally mandated defense supplier with $8.65B in confirmed, contracted backlog. The narrative lumps it with speculative nuclear names like OKLO. The data shows 2.3x annual revenue in backlog that was already signed before today. The market is pricing it as a cyclical industrial; it behaves more like a utility with contracted revenue visibility.

Watch US defense budget sequestration cut >20% (would reduce naval nuclear orders)

SaaSpocalypse — who survives Data ahead
DDOGNOWHUBSPLTRSNOWMDB

The blanket SaaS sell-off creates separation between beneficiaries and casualties. DDOG benefits from AI proliferation (more AI workloads = more observability data). HUBS is genuinely at risk (seat-based CRM is what AI agents cannibalize first). The opportunity is in companies where AI is additive to revenue, not destructive. DDOG's accelerating growth while its sector declines is the signal.

Watch HUBS Q2 2026: whether seat expansion stabilizes or accelerates the decline

Industrial / Infrastructure Rotation Data ahead
VRTETNGEVPWRHUBBEMR

VRT at 49x forward P/E carries real downside (-30-40%) if hyperscaler capex decelerates. The bear case is not wrong — it just requires a specific scenario. Against that: the $15B order book provides 18+ months of revenue visibility that doesn't evaporate in one quarter. The industrial macro rotation is institutional and persistent. The risk-reward improves the further it corrects.

Watch VRT Q2 2026: order rate and margin

Credit / Macro Posture Aligned
SPYIWMMAV

No significant divergence between the macro narrative and the credit data. HY spreads widen before recessions — they are tightening. This is a multiple compression event in growth stocks (duration sensitivity to real rates), not a credit event. The posture stays: Hold and sharpen. Preserve capital in defensible names, sharpen positions in verified dislocations.

Watch HY OAS above 4

Aviation Supercycle — Backlog Reality Data ahead
GERTXTDGHEICO

The narrative conflates 'COVID recovery complete' with 'aerospace opportunity done' — two different claims. The new structural driver is fleet renewal: the global fleet is aging, fuel efficiency mandates require new engine technology, and aviation demand in emerging markets is growing structurally. The LEAP backlog at $211B and growing while the narrative says 'industrials already ran' is the DATA_AHEAD gap. Best entry at ~35x forward PE or on an earnings-driven dislocation.

Watch GE Q2 2026 backlog total — any decline signals demand saturation

Midstream Pipelines as AI Infrastructure Income Data ahead
ETEPDWMBMPLX

The market classifies ET as an energy stock. Correct classification: AI infrastructure income vehicle with contractual fee structure. $25B in contracted fees over 18 years is not commodity exposure — it is infrastructure annuity income with an AI demand driver. The narrative says 'pipelines are boring energy stocks.' The data says ET has sold 18 years of AI infrastructure pipeline capacity at contractual rates. The misclassification is the opportunity. 7-8% yield provides income while waiting for narrative convergence.

Watch ET Q2 2026 DCF must confirm AI data center gas volume ramp — any QoQ decline changes the thesis

Sources: Fed Funds Rate: FRED FEDFUNDS · S&P 500: Shiller/Yale + Yahoo Finance (2020–2026) · Shiller CAPE: Robert Shiller / multpl.com · M2 Growth: Federal Reserve H.6 / FRED (M2SL) · HY OAS: ICE BofA / FRED from 1997; pre-1997 Moody's Baa approx · Recessions: NBER
Phase C Projection · Three Models · 2026–2030

S&P 500 Projection

Three independent models — EPS × P/E, CAPE reversion, and institutional — across base, bull, and bear scenarios.

How this projection is built: Three independent models, not analyst price targets. Model 1 (EPS-driven): Price = EPS × P/E. FactSet consensus EPS + Goldman multiple assumption. Model 2 (CAPE mean-reversion): Projects price if CAPE reverts toward its long-run average over the window. Model 3 (Institutional range): Annualised return projections from Vanguard (4–5%), Research Affiliates (3.1%), GMO (−5.4% real) translated to price levels. Rate path = FOMC SEP median (FRED FEDTARMD, Mar 2026).
Base — EPS delivers, multiple flat
Bull — EPS beats, multiple expands
Bear — CAPE mean-reverts / EPS misses
Rate (historical)
Rate (FOMC SEP)
S&P historical
Model 1: EPS × P/E
Model 2: CAPE reversion
Model 3: Institutional
Institutional band
Select a scenario to see the model assumptions and outputs.

Model comparison — 2030 S&P 500 implied price

Methodology transparency: Model 1 uses FactSet/Goldman EPS consensus × P/E — a structured calculation, not an analyst price target. Model 2 applies CAPE mean-reversion to current CAPE 41.6× with EPS growth — mathematically grounded valuation headwind. Model 3 translates Vanguard VCMM Mar 2026, Research Affiliates Jan 2026, GMO Q1 2026 10-year return forecasts into price levels. All three models are shown simultaneously — the range represents genuine uncertainty.
